title: Fixed-First Pair Alias Template
prompt_md:
## Goal
Define an alias template that fixes the first element of a `std::pair` to `std::string`, leaving the second element open. Use it to store and print two differently-typed pairs.

## Input
- Line 1: a single word and an integer, space-separated
- Line 2: a single word and a double, space-separated

## Output
Two lines:
```
<word1> <integer>
<word2> <double>
```
The double prints with default `std::cout` precision (no manipulators).

## Constraints
- Define an alias template `StringPair<T>` that expands to `std::pair<std::string, T>`.
- Use `StringPair<int>` and `StringPair<double>` to hold the data; do not write `std::pair` directly in `main`.
- Print with a function template that takes a `StringPair<T>`.

title: Coordinate Alias Template with Struct
prompt_md:
## Goal
Define a struct template for a 2D coordinate, an alias template over it, and a fully-fixed alias for the integer case, then compute the Manhattan distance between two points.

## Input
- Line 1: two integers `x1 y1` (the first point)
- Line 2: two integers `x2 y2` (the second point)

All values are in -1000..1000.

## Output
Three lines:
```
A: (<x1>, <y1>)
B: (<x2>, <y2>)
Manhattan distance: <distance>
```
where `<distance>` is `|x1 - x2| + |y1 - y2|`.

## Constraints
- Define a struct template `Coord<T>` with members `x` and `y`.
- Define an alias template `Point<T>` for `Coord<T>`, and a regular alias `IntPoint` for `Point<int>`.
- Use `IntPoint` (not `Coord<int>`) throughout `main` and in the distance function. Use `std::abs` from `<cstdlib>`.

test_cases.label: Hidden -- same point gives distance zero
